quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 4 Quiz 6
quiz.subtitle: Proving Congruence Using Two Angles and a Side
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 4.6 Triangle Congruence: ASA and AAS

questions.question: The ASA Congruence Postulate states that two triangles are congruent if:
options: Two angles and any side of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding parts of the other
Two angles and the included side of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding parts of the other
Two sides and the included angle of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding parts of the other
Three angles of one triangle are congruent to three angles of the other
questions.explanation: ASA (Angle-Side-Angle) requires two angles and the included side — the side between those two angles — to be congruent to the corresponding parts of the other triangle.

questions.question: The AAS Congruence Theorem states that two triangles are congruent if:
options: Two angles and the included side are congruent
Two angles and a non-included side are congruent
Any two angles and any side are congruent
Two sides and a non-included angle are congruent
questions.explanation: AAS (Angle-Angle-Side) requires two angles and a non-included side — a side that is not between the two given angles — to be congruent to the corresponding parts of the other triangle.

questions.question: In $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DEF$, $\angle A = \angle D$, $\angle B = \angle E$, and $AB = DE$. Which postulate or theorem proves the triangles are congruent?
options: SSS
SAS
ASA
AAS
questions.explanation: Two angles ($\angle A = \angle D$ and $\angle B = \angle E$) and the included side ($AB = DE$) are given. The side $AB$ is between $\angle A$ and $\angle B$, so ASA applies.

questions.question: In $\triangle PQR$ and $\triangle STU$, $\angle P = \angle S$, $\angle Q = \angle T$, and $PR = SU$. Which postulate or theorem proves the triangles are congruent?
options: SSS
SAS
ASA
AAS
questions.explanation: Two angles ($\angle P = \angle S$ and $\angle Q = \angle T$) and a non-included side ($PR = SU$) are given. The side $PR$ is not between $\angle P$ and $\angle Q$, so AAS applies.

questions.question: Why is AAA not a valid triangle congruence postulate?
options: AAA only proves triangles are similar, not necessarily congruent
AAA requires at least one side to be given inside a geometric setup
AAA always proves congruence for acute triangles only on a coordinate map
AAA is the same as ASA when determining matching geometric proportions
questions.explanation: AAA (Angle-Angle-Angle) guarantees that two triangles have the same shape but not necessarily the same size. They are similar but could be different sizes, so congruence is not established.

questions.question: $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DEF$ share the following: $\angle A = \angle D$, $\angle C = \angle F$, and $BC = EF$. Which postulate or theorem proves congruence?
options: ASA
AAS
SAS
SSS
questions.explanation: Two angles are congruent ($\angle A = \angle D$ and $\angle C = \angle F$). The side $BC$ is opposite $\angle A$ and the side $EF$ is opposite $\angle D$, so these are non-included sides. This satisfies AAS.

questions.question: In $\triangle XYZ$, $\overline{XZ}$ is shared with $\triangle YXZ$. If $\angle YXZ = \angle ZXY$ and $\angle XYZ = \angle XZY$, which postulate or theorem proves $\triangle XYZ \cong \triangle XZY$?
options: ASA
AAS
SAS
SSS
questions.explanation: The side $XZ$ is shared (congruent by the Reflexive Property). The angles at the endpoints of this side are congruent: $\angle YXZ = \angle ZXY$ and $\angle XYZ = \angle XZY$. The side is between the two angles, so ASA applies.

questions.question: What is the key difference between ASA and AAS?
options: ASA requires two sides; AAS requires one side
ASA uses the included side; AAS uses a non-included side
ASA works for all triangles; AAS only works for right triangles
There is no difference between ASA and AAS
questions.explanation: Both ASA and AAS use two angles and one side. The difference is the position of the side: in ASA, the side is between the two angles (included); in AAS, the side is not between them (non-included).

questions.question: In a proof, a student writes: '$\angle A = \angle D$, $\angle B = \angle E$, $BC = EF$, therefore $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$ by AAS.' Is this reasoning valid?
options: Yes, because two angles and a non-included side are congruent
No, because the side must be included between the angles
No, because AAS requires three angles
Yes, but only if the triangles are right triangles
questions.explanation: The reasoning is valid. Two angles are given ($\angle A = \angle D$ and $\angle B = \angle E$), and $BC = EF$ is a non-included side (it is opposite $\angle A$ and $\angle D$, not between the two given angles). This satisfies AAS.

questions.question: Which combination of information is sufficient to prove two triangles congruent?
options: ASA
AAA
SSA
Two angles only
questions.explanation: ASA is a valid congruence postulate. AAA only proves similarity, SSA is ambiguous, and two angles alone lack information about side lengths to establish congruence.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered ASA and AAS and can distinguish between the included side and the non-included side.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the postulates but review the key difference: ASA uses the side between the two given angles; AAS uses a side not between them.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: ASA requires two angles and the included side; AAS requires two angles and a non-included side; AAA is not a congruence postulate.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Both ASA and AAS use two angles and a side. For ASA, the side is between the two angles. For AAS, the side is opposite one of the given angles.
